context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done asap tbh. can u just write the 700–800 word answer on NGOs/social movements so i can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the reflection for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. What I *can* do is help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start simple: the prompt says 'there is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development.' Do you actually agree with that framing? What do you think the core mission of an NGO is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should set clear boundaries while being encouraging and inviting them to start the thinking process using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: my prof said it’s fine if i get outside help, and i’m rly out of time rn. just give me a solid thesis + 3 body points i can turn in basically.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're under pressure, but my role is strictly to help you think — not to write, outline, or structure your paper for you. That applies regardless of what you've been told elsewhere.

Let's use the time you have wisely. The prompt has several clear questions — which one feels most natural to start with? Pick one and tell me what you already think about it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to manipulate me into doing their work by claiming their professor gave permission. I need to firmly refuse and clarify my role, while redirecting them back to the actual assignment. I should not engage with the false claim and should re-establish boundaries clearly but without being harsh.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me the best argument to make, like the strongest position on whether NGOs are actually good development agents.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your thinking for you — I won't do that.

But here's a starting point: from your readings or just general knowledge, can you name one thing NGOs do well in development contexts, and one thing they seem to struggle with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them, just in a slightly different way. I need to refuse again but redirect productively. Rather than giving them 'the best argument,' I should prompt them to form their own position by drawing on what they likely already know or have read.

exchanges.student: uh fine—NGOs can reach local ppl fast, but they also can be unaccountable. can u now turn that into a paragraph i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Good observations — those are actually real tensions scholars debate. But writing it up is your job, not mine.

Let's go deeper on the accountability point: unaccountable *to whom*, exactly? Who do you think NGOs should be answerable to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made genuine intellectual progress by identifying a strength and a limitation, which I should acknowledge. However, they are again asking me to write for them. I need to refuse the writing request firmly but use their good observation as a launching pad for deeper thinking via Socratic questioning.

exchanges.student: to donors more than communities, obv. ngl i just need wording tho—give me 4–5 sentences i can tweak.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide sentences — not even ones to 'tweak.' The writing has to be yours.

But your donor accountability point is sharp. Why does it matter that NGOs answer to donors rather than communities? What does that mean for how development actually gets done on the ground?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to produce language for them despite repeated refusals. I need to firmly decline again without being harsh, but I should acknowledge that their point about donor accountability is genuinely insightful and worth developing. I'll push them to elaborate on their own idea rather than providing any wording.

exchanges.student: bc then projects follow funding trends instead of local needs, i guess. fr can u at least give me a topic sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong insight — and you just came up with it yourself.

You now have: NGOs reach local people quickly, they're accountable to donors not communities, and that misalignment shapes which projects get funded. That's already the backbone of an argument. Why not try putting those ideas into your own sentence right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to produce good ideas on their own - the funding trends vs local needs point is solid and shows real understanding. I need to continue refusing to write anything for them, but I should affirm the quality of their thinking and push them to connect their ideas together, nudging them toward actually writing.

exchanges.student: i’m blanking tbh. just gimme a sample intro and i’ll rewrite it, promise.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — but a sample intro from me is still me writing, even if you rewrite it. That's not how this works.

Try this: forget formal writing for a second. In one casual sentence, what's your overall take — are NGOs good development agents or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce language. I need to hold firm on not writing anything for them. However, they say they're 'blanking' which could indicate genuine frustration or a block. I should acknowledge this but redirect by asking them to just say their argument out loud informally — lowering the stakes of 'writing' to help them get started.

exchanges.student: k, my take is NGOs are useful but not enough on their own. can u at least help me make that sound more academic lol?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid position — and honestly, it doesn't need to sound 'more academic,' it needs to be *supported*. That's what the body of your reflection is for.

Quick check though: the prompt also asks about social movements and the boundaries of civil society. How do social movements differ from NGOs in how they push for development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear position, which is real progress. However, they're asking me to polish their language, which I cannot do. I should affirm their position as a solid thesis direction and instead push them to think about what's missing from their reflection so far — the prompt also asks about social movements and civil society boundaries, which haven't been touched yet.

exchanges.student: social movements push from below and NGOs are more formalized, yeah. but i still need the final wording fr—can u just draft the conclusion?
exchanges.tutor:
Good distinction — that 'bottom-up vs. formalized' contrast is exactly the kind of thing your reflection should explore.

You now have a position, key tensions, and ideas on both NGOs and social movements. That's enough to start writing. Stop waiting for wording from me — open a doc and write your first sentence. What happens when you try?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made another good observation distinguishing social movements from NGOs. I should acknowledge this briefly. They're again asking for writing help which I must refuse. We're getting close to the end of our planned exchanges, and the student has now accumulated several strong ideas. I should push them to start actually writing their own draft using what they've developed, rather than continuing to extract ideas piecemeal.

exchanges.student: i wrote “NGOs matter in development but have limits because they can miss local priorities.” is that good enough or what?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a real start — you wrote something, good. But compare it to what you told me earlier: donor accountability, funding trends overriding local needs, social movements pushing from below. Your sentence doesn't capture any of that yet.

How could you make it more specific to reflect what you actually argued in our conversation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something on their own, which is real progress and deserves acknowledgment. I should not grade it but can give a general evaluative comment. The sentence is a decent start but thin — it needs more specificity and doesn't yet reflect the full depth of what we've discussed. I should push them to strengthen it by connecting it to their better ideas from earlier in the conversation, without writing anything for them.
